html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Eleven — Part Three

The Book of Shofetim

SHOFETIM

The Book of Judges

Chapters 15 through 21

Shimshon's End — Mika's Shrine — The Outrage of Giv'ah — Every Man Did What Was Right in His Own Eyes

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QJudga (4Q49) — Cave 4 — Primary DSS witness for Shofetim

4QJudgb (4Q50) — Cave 4 — Supporting DSS fragments

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — Shofetim preserved in full

YHWH and The Creator only — no substitute titles

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHOFETIM — CHAPTERS 15 THROUGH 21 — SHIMSHON AND THE APPENDICES

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 15 Shimshon and the Foxes — Jawbone of a Donkey — A Thousand Men

1 After some days, at the time of wheat harvest, Shimshon went to visit his wife with a young goat. And he said, I will go in to my wife in the chamber. But her father would not allow him to go in.

2 And her father said, I really thought that you utterly hated her, so I gave her to your companion. Is not her younger sister more beautiful than she? Please take her instead.

3 And Shimshon said to them, This time I shall be innocent in regard to the Pelishtim, when I do them harm.

4 So Shimshon went and caught three hundred foxes and took torches. And he turned them tail to tail and put one torch between each pair of tails.

5 And when he had set fire to the torches, he let the foxes go into the standing grain of the Pelishtim and set fire to the stacked grain and the standing grain, as well as the olive orchards.

6 Then the Pelishtim said, Who has done this? And they said, Shimshon, the son-in-law of the Timnite, because he has taken his wife and given her to his companion. And the Pelishtim came up and burned her and her father with fire.

7 And Shimshon said to them, If this is what you do, I swear I will be avenged on you, and after that I will quit.

8 And he struck them hip and thigh with a great blow, and he went down and stayed in the cleft of the rock of Etam.

9 Then the Pelishtim came up and encamped in Yehudah and made a raid on Lehi.

10 And the men of Yehudah said, Why have you come up against us? They said, We have come up to bind Shimshon, to do to him as he did to us.

11 Then three thousand men of Yehudah went down to the cleft of the rock of Etam, and said to Shimshon, Do you not know that the Pelishtim are rulers over us? What then is this that you have done to us? And he said to them, As they did to me, so have I done to them.

12 And they said to him, We have come down to bind you, that we may give you into the hands of the Pelishtim. And Shimshon said to them, Swear to me that you will not attack me yourselves.

13 They said to him, No, we will only bind you and give you into their hands. We will surely not kill you. So they bound him with two new ropes and brought him up from the rock.

14 When he came to Lehi, the Pelishtim came shouting to meet him. Then the Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and the ropes that were on his arms became as flax that has caught fire, and his bonds melted off his hands.

15 And he found a fresh jawbone of a donkey, and put out his hand and took it, and with it he struck one thousand men.

16 And Shimshon said, With the jawbone of a donkey, heaps upon heaps, with the jawbone of a donkey have I struck down a thousand men.

17 As soon as he had finished speaking, he threw away the jawbone out of his hand. And that place was called Ramat-Lehi.

18 And he was very thirsty, and he called upon YHWH and said, You have granted this great salvation by the hand of your servant, and shall I now die of thirst and fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?

19 And Creator split open the hollow place that is at Lehi, and water came out from it. And when he drank, his spirit returned, and he revived. Therefore the name of it was called En-Hakkore — it is at Lehi to this day.

20 And he judged Yisra'EL in the days of the Pelishtim twenty years.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 15. The Spirit of YHWH rushing upon Shimshon at the moment of greatest constraint — verse 14 — is the consistent pattern of Shimshon's empowerment: the Spirit operates at the point of human impossibility. The ropes that bound him become flax set on fire.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 15 in full. Shimshon's prayer in verse 18 — you have granted this great salvation by the hand of your servant, and shall I now die of thirst — is one of his few recorded prayers and one of the most honest: the acknowledgment of YHWH's salvation paired with the immediate acknowledgment of his own need.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 15. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Verse 20 — he judged Yisra'EL twenty years — is the formal closing formula of the Shimshon cycle, though the narrative continues for two more chapters, making Shimshon the only judge whose death follows the closing formula.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — do you not know that the Pelishtim are rulers over us? The men of Yehudah have accepted Pelishti dominion as the normal condition. They are not crying out to YHWH — they are managing their subjugation. When Shimshon disrupts the Pelishti order, it is Yehudah that comes to hand him over. The covenant community has become so accommodated to oppression that the deliverer is their problem rather than their hope. Verse 14 — the Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and the ropes became as flax set on fire. The covenant irony is complete: three thousand men of Yehudah bound the judge to deliver him to the enemy, and YHWH burns the bonds away at the moment of greatest covenant failure.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 16 Shimshon and Delilah — He Did Not Know That YHWH Had Left Him — Shimshon's Death

1 Shimshon went to Azzah, and there he saw a prostitute, and he went in to her.

2 The Azzahites were told, Shimshon has come here. And they surrounded the place and set an ambush for him all night at the gate of the city. They kept quiet all night, saying, In the morning, when it is light, we will kill him.

3 But Shimshon lay till midnight, and at midnight he arose and took hold of the doors of the gate of the city and the two posts, and pulled them up, bar and all, and put them on his shoulders and carried them to the top of the hill that is in front of Khevron.

4 After this he loved a woman in the Valley of Sorek, whose name was Delilah.

5 And the lords of the Pelishtim came up to her and said to her, Seduce him, and see where his great strength lies, and by what means we may overpower him, that we may bind him to humble him. And we will each give you eleven hundred pieces of silver.

6 So Delilah said to Shimshon, Please tell me where your great strength lies, and how you might be bound, that one could subdue you.

7 Shimshon said to her, If they bind me with seven fresh bowstrings that have not been dried, I shall become weak and be like any other man.

8 Then the lords of the Pelishtim brought up to her seven fresh bowstrings that had not been dried, and she bound him with them.

9 Now she had men lying in ambush in an inner chamber. And she said to him, The Pelishtim are upon you, Shimshon. But he snapped the bowstrings, as a string of tow snaps when it touches the fire. So the secret of his strength was not known.

10 Then Delilah said to Shimshon, Behold, you have mocked me and told me lies. Please tell me how you might be bound.

11 And he said to her, If they bind me with new ropes that have not been used, I shall become weak and be like any other man.

12 So Delilah took new ropes and bound him with them and said to him, The Pelishtim are upon you, Shimshon. And the men lying in ambush were in an inner chamber. But he snapped the ropes off his arms like a thread.

13 Then Delilah said to Shimshon, Until now you have mocked me and told me lies. Tell me how you might be bound. And he said to her, If you weave the seven locks of my head with the web and fasten it tight with the pin, then I shall become weak and be like any other man.

14 So while he slept, Delilah took the seven locks of his head and wove them into the web. And she made them tight with the pin and said to him, The Pelishtim are upon you, Shimshon. But he awoke from his sleep and pulled away the pin, the loom, and the web.

15 And she said to him, How can you say, I love you, when your heart is not with me? You have mocked me these three times, and you have not told me where your great strength lies.

16 And when she pressed him hard with her words day after day, and urged him, his soul was vexed to death.

17 And he told her all his heart, and said to her, A razor has never come upon my head, for I have been a Nazirite to Creator from my mother's womb. If my head is shaved, then my strength will leave me, and I shall become weak and be like any other man.

18 When Delilah saw that he had told her all his heart, she sent and called the lords of the Pelishtim, saying, Come up again, for he has told me all his heart. Then the lords of the Pelishtim came up to her and brought the money in their hands.

19 She made him sleep on her knees. And she called a man and had him shave off the seven locks of his head. Then she began to torment him, and his strength left him.

20 And she said, The Pelishtim are upon you, Shimshon. And he awoke from his sleep and said, I will go out as at other times and shake myself free. But he did not know that YHWH had left him.

21 And the Pelishtim seized him and gouged out his eyes and brought him down to Azzah and bound him with bronze shackles. And he ground at the mill in the prison.

22 But the hair of his head began to grow again after it had been shaved.

23 Now the lords of the Pelishtim gathered to offer a great sacrifice to Dagon their god and to rejoice, and they said, Our god has given Shimshon our enemy into our hand.

24 And when the people saw him, they praised their god. For they said, Our god has given our enemy into our hand, the ravager of our country, who has killed many of us.

25 And when their hearts were merry, they said, Call Shimshon, that he may entertain us. So they called Shimshon out of the prison, and he entertained them. They made him stand between the pillars.

26 And Shimshon said to the young man who held him by the hand, Let me feel the pillars on which the house rests, that I may lean against them.

27 Now the house was full of men and women. All the lords of the Pelishtim were there, and on the roof there were about three thousand men and women, who looked on while Shimshon entertained.

28 Then Shimshon called to YHWH and said, O YHWH Creator, please remember me and please strengthen me only this once, O Creator, that I may be avenged on the Pelishtim for my two eyes.

29 And Shimshon grasped the two middle pillars on which the house rested, and he leaned his weight against them, his right hand on the one and his left hand on the other.

30 And Shimshon said, Let me die with the Pelishtim. Then he bowed with all his strength, and the house fell upon the lords and upon all the people who were in it. So the dead whom he killed at his death were more than those whom he had killed during his life.

31 Then his brothers and all his family came down and took him and brought him up and buried him between Tzorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of Manoakh his father. He had judged Yisra'EL twenty years.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 16. Verse 20 — he did not know that YHWH had left him — is one of the most tragic verses in the entire Nevi'im. Shimshon's confidence in his own strength had become so habitual that he had ceased to recognize it as YHWH's gift. When the gift was withdrawn, he reached for it as if it were still his.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 16 in full. Shimshon's final prayer — verse 28 — please remember me and please strengthen me only this once — is read as one of the most concentrated covenant cries in Shofetim: the broken judge asking YHWH for one more act of covenant faithfulness despite his own covenant failures.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 16. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Verse 30 — the dead whom he killed at his death were more than those he had killed during his life — is the covenant summary of Shimshon's greatest act performed in his weakest and most defeated state.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 20 — he did not know that YHWH had left him. This is the theological center of the entire Shimshon narrative. The gradual erosion of the Nazirite vow — first the honey from the carcass violating the purity laws, then the seven disclosures approaching but not quite revealing the truth to Delilah, then the final disclosure — each step a further move away from covenant faithfulness. And throughout, the strength remained — until it did not. Verse 28 — please remember me and please strengthen me only this once. The prayer is personal and honest. YHWH answers it. The judge who lived by covenant violation dies by covenant faithfulness — his greatest act accomplished in blindness and chains.

THE APPENDICES — CHAPTERS 17 THROUGH 21 — COVENANT COLLAPSE

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 17 Mika's Shrine — A Levite Becomes His Priest — Every Man Did What Was Right in His Own Eyes

1 There was a man of the hill country of Efrayim, whose name was Mika.

2 And he said to his mother, The eleven hundred pieces of silver that were taken from you, about which you uttered a curse, and also spoke it in my ears — behold, the silver is with me — I took it. And his mother said, Blessed be my son by YHWH.

3 And he restored the eleven hundred pieces of silver to his mother. And his mother said, I dedicate the silver to YHWH from my hand for my son, to make a carved image and a metal image. Now therefore I will restore it to you.

4 So when he restored the money to his mother, his mother took two hundred pieces of silver and gave it to the silversmith, who made it into a carved image and a metal image. And it was in the house of Mika.

5 And the man Mika had a shrine, and he made an ephod and household gods, and ordained one of his sons, who became his priest.

6 In those days there was no king in Yisra'EL. Every man did what was right in his own eyes.

7 Now there was a young man of Beit-Lekhem in Yehudah, of the family of Yehudah, who was a Levite, and he sojourned there.

8 And the man departed from the town of Beit-Lekhem in Yehudah to sojourn where he could find a place. And as he journeyed, he came to the hill country of Efrayim to the house of Mika.

9 And Mika said to him, Where do you come from? And he said to him, I am a Levite of Beit-Lekhem in Yehudah, and I am going to sojourn where I may find a place.

10 And Mika said to him, Stay with me, and be to me a father and a priest, and I will give you ten pieces of silver a year and a suit of clothes and your living. And the Levite went in.

11 And the Levite was content to dwell with the man, and the young man became to him like one of his sons.

12 And Mika ordained the Levite, and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Mika.

13 Then Mika said, Now I know that YHWH will prosper me, because I have a Levite as priest.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 17. The Mika narrative — Chapters 17-18 — introduces the appendices of Shofetim, which document the full depth of covenant collapse. The refrain of verse 6 — in those days there was no king in Yisra'EL — every man did what was right in his own eyes — is the theological caption for the entire closing section.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 17 in full. Verse 13 — now I know that YHWH will prosper me, because I have a Levite as priest — is read as the paradigm of religious self-deception: Mika constructs an entirely unauthorized shrine, installs a hired Levite, and believes YHWH's blessing will follow his own invention.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 17. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Mika's shrine includes the full apparatus of unauthorized worship: carved image, metal image, ephod, household gods, and a privately ordained priest. Each element violates Mosaic Torah.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — every man did what was right in his own eyes. The same formula will close the entire book in 21:25. It is not a description of freedom — it is a diagnosis of covenant anarchy. The absence of covenant governance does not produce human flourishing but the collapse of the covenant order into individual self-determination. Mika's mother invokes YHWH's blessing over the creation of an idol. Mika mistakes the Levite's presence for covenant legitimacy. Every element of the story is sincere and every element is covenant violation. The characters are not cynical — they are deceived. The most dangerous religious error is the one done in good faith.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 18 The Danites Steal Mika's Priest and Idols — The Tribe of Dan Takes Leshem

1 In those days there was no king in Yisra'EL. And in those days the tribe of the people of Dan was seeking for itself an inheritance to dwell in, for until then no inheritance among the tribes of Yisra'EL had fallen to them.

2 So the people of Dan sent five able men from the whole number of their tribe, from Tzorah and from Eshtaol, to spy out the land and to explore it. And they said to them, Go and explore the land. And they came to the hill country of Efrayim, to the house of Mika, and lodged there.

3 When they were by the house of Mika, they recognized the voice of the young Levite. And they turned aside and said to him, Who brought you here? What are you doing in this place? What is your business here?

4 And he said to them, This is how Mika dealt with me: he has hired me, and I have become his priest.

5 And they said to him, Inquire of Creator, please, that we may know whether the journey on which we are going will succeed.

6 And the priest said to them, Go in peace. The journey on which you go is under the eye of YHWH.

7 Then the five men departed and came to Laish and saw the people who were there, how they lived in security, after the manner of the Tzidonians, quiet and unsuspecting, lacking nothing that is in the earth and possessing wealth, and how they were far from the Tzidonians and had no dealings with anyone.

8 And when they came to their brothers at Tzorah and Eshtaol, their brothers said to them, What do you report?

9 They said, Arise, and let us go up against them, for we have seen the land, and behold, it is very good. And will you do nothing? Do not be slow to go, to enter in and possess the land.

10 As soon as you go, you will come to an unsuspecting people. The land is spacious, for Creator has given it into your hands, a place where there is no lack of anything that is in the earth.

11 So from there the clan of Dan, six hundred men armed with weapons of war, set out from Tzorah and Eshtaol,

12 And they went up and encamped at Kiryat-Ye'arim in Yehudah. On this account that place is called Makhaneh-Dan to this day — behold, it is west of Kiryat-Ye'arim.

13 And they passed on from there to the hill country of Efrayim, and came to the house of Mika.

14 Then the five men who had gone to scout out the country of Laish said to their brothers, Do you know that in these houses there are an ephod, household gods, a carved image, and a metal image? Now therefore consider what you will do.

15 And they turned aside there and came to the house of the young Levite, at the home of Mika, and asked him about his welfare.

16 Now the six hundred men of the Danites, armed with their weapons of war, stood by the entrance of the gate.

17 And the five men who had gone to scout out the land went up and entered and took the carved image, the ephod, the household gods, and the metal image, while the priest stood by the entrance of the gate with the six hundred men armed with weapons of war.

18 And when these went into Mika's house and took the carved image, the ephod, the household gods, and the metal image, the priest said to them, What are you doing?

19 And they said to him, Keep quiet — put your hand on your mouth and come with us and be to us a father and a priest. Is it better for you to be priest to the house of one man, or to be priest to a tribe and clan in Yisra'EL?

20 And the priest's heart was glad. He took the ephod and the household gods and the carved image and went among the people.

21 So they turned and departed, putting the little ones and the livestock and the goods in front of them.

22 When they had gone a distance from the home of Mika, the men who were in the houses near Mika's house were called out, and they overtook the people of Dan.

23 And they shouted to the people of Dan, who turned around and said to Mika, What is the matter with you, that you come with such a company?

24 And he said, You take my gods that I made and the priest, and go away, and what have I left? How then do you ask me, What is the matter with you?

25 And the people of Dan said to him, Do not let your voice be heard among us, lest angry fellows fall upon you, and you lose your life with the lives of your household.

26 Then the people of Dan went their way. And when Mika saw that they were too strong for him, he turned and went back to his home.

27 But the people of Dan took what Mika had made, and the priest who belonged to him, and they came to Laish, to a people quiet and unsuspecting, and struck them with the edge of the sword and burned the city with fire.

28 And there was no deliverer because it was far from Tzidon, and they had no dealings with anyone. It was in the valley that belongs to Beit-Rekhov. And they rebuilt the city and lived in it.

29 And they named the city Dan, after the name of Dan their ancestor, who was born to Yisra'EL — though the name of the city was Laish at the first.

30 And the people of Dan set up the carved image for themselves, and Yehonatan the son of Gershom, son of Moshe, and his sons were priests to the tribe of Dan until the day of the captivity of the land.

31 So they set up Mika's carved image that he made, as long as the house of Creator was at Shiloh.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 18. The Dan migration narrative is one of the most theologically significant passages in the Shofetim appendices. Verse 30 reveals that the priest who served Mika's idol was Yehonatan the son of Gershom, son of Moshe — a grandson of Moshe himself serving an illegitimate shrine.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 18 in full. The Danites' theft of Mika's idols and priest is read as the covenant demonstration that what is built on covenant violation cannot be protected: Mika made his gods and they were taken from him by those stronger than he.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 18. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Verse 30 — Yehonatan son of Gershom son of Moshe — is one of the most jarring revelations in the entire Nevi'im: a direct descendant of Moshe serving as priest for an idolatrous shrine.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 30 — Yehonatan son of Gershom son of Moshe. The Masoretic scribes inserted a suspended letter nun into the name, changing Moshe to Menasheh — apparently to protect Moshe's honor. The DSS reading and the plain Hebrew text support Moshe. The grandson of the greatest covenant figure in Yisra'EL's history became the priest of an illegitimate Danite shrine. This is the theological climax of the Mika narrative: covenant failure is not confined to the covenant community's edges. It reaches into the closest descendants of the covenant's greatest servant. Verse 24 — you take my gods that I made. Mika's grief over his stolen idols is real — and the text's irony is devastating: you take my gods that I made. The god he made was taken. YHWH cannot be made or taken.

THE OUTRAGE OF GIV'AH AND THE WAR AGAINST BINYAMIN — CHAPTERS 19 THROUGH 21

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 19 The Levite and His Concubine in Giv'ah — The Outrage in Binyamin

1 In those days, when there was no king in Yisra'EL, a certain Levite was sojourning in the remote parts of the hill country of Efrayim, who took to himself a concubine from Beit-Lekhem in Yehudah.

2 And his concubine was unfaithful to him, and she went away from him to her father's house at Beit-Lekhem in Yehudah, and was there some four months.

3 Then her husband arose and went after her, to speak kindly to her and bring her back. He had with him his servant and a couple of donkeys. And she brought him into her father's house. And when the girl's father saw him, he came with joy to meet him.

4 And his father-in-law, the girl's father, made him stay, and he remained with him three days. So they ate and drank and spent the night there.

5 And on the fourth day they arose early in the morning, and he prepared to go, but the girl's father said to his son-in-law, Strengthen your heart with a morsel of bread, and after that you may go.

6 So the two of them sat and ate and drank together. And the girl's father said to the man, Be pleased to spend the night, and let your heart be merry.

7 And when the man rose up to go, his father-in-law pressed him, till he spent the night there again.

8 And on the fifth day he arose early in the morning to depart. And the girl's father said, Strengthen your heart and wait until the day declines. So they ate, both of them.

9 And when the man and his concubine and his servant rose up to depart, his father-in-law, the girl's father, said to him, Behold, now the day has waned toward evening. Please, spend the night. Behold, the day draws to its close. Lodge here and let your heart be merry, and tomorrow you shall arise early in the morning for your journey, and go home.

10 But the man would not spend the night. He rose up and departed and arrived opposite Yevus — that is, Yerushalayim. He had with him a couple of saddled donkeys, and his concubine was with him.

11 When they were near Yevus, the day was nearly over, and the servant said to his master, Come now, let us turn aside to this city of the Yevusites and spend the night in it.

12 And his master said to him, We will not turn aside into the city of foreigners, who do not belong to the people of Yisra'EL, but we will pass on to Giv'ah.

13 And he said to his servant, Come and let us draw near to one of these places and spend the night at Giv'ah or at Ramah.

14 So they passed on and went their way. And the sun went down on them near Giv'ah, which belongs to Binyamin,

15 And they turned aside there, to go in and spend the night at Giv'ah. And he went in and sat down in the open square of the city, for no one took them into his house to spend the night.

16 And behold, an old man was coming from his work in the field at evening. The man was from the hill country of Efrayim, and he was sojourning in Giv'ah. The men of the place were Binyaminites.

17 And he lifted up his eyes and saw the traveler in the open square of the city. And the old man said, Where are you going? And where do you come from?

18 And he said to him, We are passing from Beit-Lekhem in Yehudah to the remote parts of the hill country of Efrayim, from which I come. I went to Beit-Lekhem in Yehudah, and I am going to the house of YHWH, but no one has taken me into his house.

19 We have straw and feed for our donkeys, with bread and wine for me and your female servant and the young man with your servants. There is no lack of anything.

20 And the old man said, Peace be to you. I will care for all your wants. Only, do not spend the night in the square.

21 So he brought him into his house and gave the donkeys feed. And they washed their feet, and ate and drank.

22 As they were making their hearts merry, behold, the men of the city, worthless fellows, surrounded the house, beating on the door. And they said to the old man, the master of the house, Bring out the man who came into your house, that we may know him.

23 And the man, the master of the house, went out to them and said to them, No, my brothers, do not act so wickedly — since this man has come into my house, do not do this outrage.

24 Here are my virgin daughter and his concubine. Let me bring them out now. Violate them and do with them what seems good to you, but against this man do not do this outrageous thing.

25 But the men would not listen to him. So the man seized his concubine and made her go out to them. And they knew her and abused her all night until the morning. And as the dawn began to break, they let her go.

26 And as morning appeared, the woman came and fell down at the door of the man's house where her master was, until it was light.

27 And her master rose up in the morning, and when he opened the doors of the house and went out to go on his way, behold, there was his concubine lying at the door of the house, with her hands on the threshold.

28 He said to her, Get up, let us be going. But there was no answer. Then he put her on the donkey, and the man rose up and went away to his home.

29 And when he entered his house, he took a knife, and taking hold of his concubine he divided her, limb by limb, into twelve pieces, and sent her throughout all the territory of Yisra'EL.

30 And all who saw it said, Such a thing has never happened or been seen from the day that the people of Yisra'EL came up out of the land of Egypt until this day. Consider it, take counsel, and speak.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 19. The Giv'ah outrage is the most disturbing narrative in Shofetim and among the most disturbing in the entire Nevi'im. Its structural parallel to the Sodom narrative of Bereshit 19 is deliberate — Giv'ah, a city of Yisra'EL, has become Sodom.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 19 in full. The unnamed concubine's death and the Levite's sending of her body in twelve pieces throughout Yisra'EL — verse 29 — is read as the most desperate covenant alarm in the book: the body of the covenant people's most violated member becomes the call to covenant assembly.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 19. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The Levite's choice to bypass Yevus — verse 12 — because it is a city of foreigners who do not belong to Yisra'EL, preferring Giv'ah — a city of Yisra'EL — is the terrible irony of the chapter: the foreign city would have been safer.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Giv'ah narrative is the covenant inverse of the Sodom story. In Bereshit 19, the covenant community outside Sodom sends messengers into the city. In Shofetim 19, the covenant community inside Giv'ah perpetrates what the Sodomites attempted. Verse 30 — such a thing has never happened or been seen from the day that the people of Yisra'EL came up out of Egypt until this day. The comparison to Egypt is the book's own assessment of the covenant descent: the people who came out of slavery have replicated the worst of what they escaped. The refrain — in those days there was no king in Yisra'EL — is the covenant explanation: without covenant governance, the covenant community does not return to neutral — it descends.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 20 All Yisra'EL Assembles Against Giv'ah — The War Against Binyamin — Twenty-Five Thousand Slain

1 Then all the people of Yisra'EL came out, from Dan to Be'er-Sheva, including the land of Gil'ad, and the congregation assembled as one man to YHWH at Mitzpah.

2 And the chiefs of all the people, of all the tribes of Yisra'EL, presented themselves in the assembly of the people of Creator, four hundred thousand men on foot who drew the sword.

3 Now the people of Binyamin heard that the people of Yisra'EL had gone up to Mitzpah. And the people of Yisra'EL said, Tell us, how did this evil happen?

4 And the Levite, the husband of the woman who was murdered, answered and said, I came to Giv'ah that belongs to Binyamin, I and my concubine, to spend the night.

5 And the leaders of Giv'ah rose against me and surrounded the house against me by night. They intended to kill me, and they violated my concubine so that she died.

6 Then I took my concubine and cut her in pieces and sent her throughout all the country of the inheritance of Yisra'EL, for they have committed abomination and outrage in Yisra'EL.

7 Behold, you people of Yisra'EL, all of you, give your advice and counsel here.

8 And all the people arose as one man, saying, None of us will go to his tent, and none of us will return to his house.

9 But now this is what we will do to Giv'ah: we will go up against it by lot,

10 And we will take ten men of a hundred throughout all the tribes of Yisra'EL, and a hundred of a thousand, and a thousand of ten thousand, to bring provisions for the people, that when they come they may repay Giv'ah of Binyamin, for all the outrage that they have committed in Yisra'EL.

11 So all the men of Yisra'EL gathered against the city, united as one man.

12 And the tribes of Yisra'EL sent men through all the tribe of Binyamin, saying, What evil is this that has taken place among you?

13 Now therefore give up the men, the worthless fellows in Giv'ah, that we may put them to death and purge evil from Yisra'EL. But the Binyaminites would not listen to the voice of their brothers, the people of Yisra'EL.

14 Then the people of Binyamin came together out of the cities to Giv'ah to go out to battle against the people of Yisra'EL.

15 And the people of Binyamin mustered out of their cities on that day twenty-six thousand men who drew the sword, besides the inhabitants of Giv'ah, who mustered seven hundred chosen men.

16 Among all these were seven hundred chosen men who were left-handed — every one could sling a stone at a hair and not miss.

17 And the men of Yisra'EL, apart from Binyamin, mustered four hundred thousand men who drew the sword — all these were men of war.

18 The people of Yisra'EL arose and went up to Beit-El and inquired of Creator. And the people of Yisra'EL said, Who shall go up first for us to fight against the people of Binyamin? And YHWH said, Yehudah shall go up first.

19 Then the people of Yisra'EL rose in the morning and encamped against Giv'ah.

20 And the men of Yisra'EL went out to fight against Binyamin, and the men of Yisra'EL drew up the battle line against them at Giv'ah.

21 The people of Binyamin came out of Giv'ah and cut down on that day twenty-two thousand men of Yisra'EL.

22 But the people, the men of Yisra'EL, took courage and again formed the battle line in the same place where they had formed it on the first day.

23 And the people of Yisra'EL went up and wept before YHWH until the evening. And they inquired of YHWH, Shall we again draw near to fight against our brothers, the people of Binyamin? And YHWH said, Go up against them.

24 So the people of Yisra'EL came near against the people of Binyamin the second day.

25 And Binyamin went against them out of Giv'ah the second day, and cut down eighteen thousand men of the people of Yisra'EL. All these were men who drew the sword.

26 Then all the people of Yisra'EL, the whole army, went up and came to Beit-El and wept. They sat there before YHWH and fasted that day until evening, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before YHWH.

27 And the people of Yisra'EL inquired of YHWH — for the ark of the covenant of Creator was there in those days,

28 And Pinkhas the son of Ele'azar, son of Aharon, ministered before it in those days — saying, Shall we go out once more to battle against our brothers, the people of Binyamin, or shall we cease? And YHWH said, Go up, for tomorrow I will give them into your hand.

29 So Yisra'EL set men in ambush around Giv'ah.

30 And the people of Yisra'EL went up against the people of Binyamin on the third day and set themselves in array against Giv'ah, as at other times.

31 And the people of Binyamin went out against the people and were drawn away from the city. And as at other times they began to strike and kill some of the people in the highways, one of which goes up to Beit-El and the other to Giv'ah, and in the open country, about thirty men of Yisra'EL.

32 And the people of Binyamin said, They are routed before us, as at the first. But the people of Yisra'EL said, Let us flee and draw them away from the city to the highways.

33 And all the men of Yisra'EL rose up out of their place and set themselves in array at Ba'al-Tamar, and the men of Yisra'EL who were in ambush rushed out of their place from Ma'areh-Geva.

34 And there came against Giv'ah ten thousand chosen men out of all Yisra'EL, and the battle was hard. But the Binyaminites did not know that disaster was close upon them.

35 And YHWH defeated Binyamin before Yisra'EL, and the people of Yisra'EL destroyed twenty-five thousand one hundred men of Binyamin that day. All these were men who drew the sword.

36 So the people of Binyamin saw that they were defeated. And the men of Yisra'EL gave ground to Binyamin, because they trusted to the men in ambush whom they had set against Giv'ah.

37 Then the men in ambush hurried and rushed against Giv'ah. The men in ambush moved out and struck all the city with the edge of the sword.

38 Now the appointed signal between the men of Yisra'EL and the men in ambush was that when they made a great cloud of smoke rise up out of the city

39 The men of Yisra'EL should turn in battle. Now Binyamin had begun to strike and kill about thirty men of Yisra'EL. They said, Surely they are defeated before us, as in the first battle.

40 But when the signal began to rise out of the city in a column of smoke, the Binyaminites looked behind them, and behold, the whole of the city went up in smoke to heaven.

41 Then the men of Yisra'EL turned, and the men of Binyamin were dismayed, for they saw that disaster was close upon them.

42 Therefore they turned their backs before the men of Yisra'EL in the direction of the wilderness, but the battle overtook them. And those who came out of the cities were destroying them in their midst.

43 Surrounding the Binyaminites, they pursued them and trod them down from Noh'ah as far as opposite Giv'ah on the east.

44 Eighteen thousand men of Binyamin fell, all of them men of valor.

45 And they turned and fled toward the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon. Five thousand men of them were cut down in the highways. And they were pursued hard to Gidom, and two thousand men of them were struck down.

46 So all who fell that day of Binyamin were twenty-five thousand men who drew the sword, all of them men of valor.

47 But six hundred men turned and fled toward the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon and remained at the rock of Rimmon four months.

48 And the men of Yisra'EL turned back against the people of Binyamin and struck them with the edge of the sword, the city, men and beasts and all that they found. And all the towns that they found they set on fire.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 20. The intertribal war against Binyamin is the most extensive military narrative in Shofetim and among the most theologically disturbing: Yisra'EL asks YHWH's direction — verse 18, 23, 28 — yet loses forty thousand men in two days before finally prevailing on the third.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 20 in full. The three-day pattern of inquiry, defeat, defeat, and final victory is read as the covenant discipline of YHWH: even when the cause is just and the inquiry is genuine, YHWH does not always give immediate victory. The covenant community is refined through the experience of loss before the deliverance comes.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 20. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The presence of Pinkhas son of Ele'azar son of Aharon — verse 28 — provides a chronological anchor: the events of the Shofetim appendices occurred early in the period, close to the death of the Mosaic generation.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 18, 23, 28 — Yisra'EL inquires of YHWH three times, and YHWH answers three times. Yet they lose forty thousand men before the victory. The covenant principle: YHWH's direction does not guarantee immediate ease. Even when Yisra'EL is on the right side of a just cause — punishing the outrage of Giv'ah — the path to covenant justice passes through suffering. YHWH's yes does not mean an effortless victory. Verse 48 — they set on fire all the towns they found. The destruction of Binyamin almost annihilates the tribe entirely — six hundred survivors remain — verse 47. A tribe of Yisra'EL nearly ceases to exist because of the covenant failure of a single city within it.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 21 Wives for Binyamin — Every Man Did What Was Right in His Own Eyes — The Book Closes

1 Now the men of Yisra'EL had sworn at Mitzpah, No one of us shall give his daughter in marriage to Binyamin.

2 And the people came to Beit-El and sat there till evening before Creator, and they lifted up their voices and wept bitterly.

3 And they said, O YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, why has this happened in Yisra'EL, that today there should be one tribe lacking in Yisra'EL?

4 And the next day the people rose early and built there an altar and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings.

5 And the people of Yisra'EL said, Which of all the tribes of Yisra'EL did not come up in the assembly to YHWH? For they had taken a great oath concerning him who did not come up to YHWH to Mitzpah, saying, He shall surely be put to death.

6 And the people of Yisra'EL had compassion for Binyamin their brother and said, One tribe is cut off from Yisra'EL this day.

7 What shall we do for wives for those who are left, since we have sworn by YHWH that we will not give them any of our daughters for wives?

8 And they said, What one is there of the tribes of Yisra'EL that did not come up to YHWH to Mitzpah? And behold, no one had come to the camp from Yavesh-Gil'ad, to the assembly.

9 For when the people were mustered, behold, not one of the inhabitants of Yavesh-Gil'ad was there.

10 So the congregation sent twelve thousand of their bravest men there and commanded them, Go and strike the inhabitants of Yavesh-Gil'ad with the edge of the sword — also the women and the little ones.

11 This is what you shall do: every male and every woman that has lain with a male you shall devote to destruction.

12 And they found among the inhabitants of Yavesh-Gil'ad four hundred young virgins who had not known a man by lying with him, and they brought them to the camp at Shiloh, which is in the land of Kena'an.

13 Then the whole congregation sent word to the people of Binyamin who were at the rock of Rimmon and proclaimed peace to them.

14 And Binyamin returned at that time. And they gave them the women whom they had saved alive of the women of Yavesh-Gil'ad, but they were not enough for them.

15 And the people had compassion on Binyamin because YHWH had made a breach in the tribes of Yisra'EL.

16 Then the elders of the congregation said, What shall we do for wives for those who are left, since the women are destroyed out of Binyamin?

17 And they said, There must be an inheritance for the survivors of Binyamin, that a tribe not be blotted out from Yisra'EL.

18 Yet we cannot give them wives from our daughters. For the people of Yisra'EL had sworn, Cursed be he who gives a wife to Binyamin.

19 So they said, Behold, there is the yearly feast of YHWH at Shiloh, which is north of Beit-El, on the east of the highway that goes up from Beit-El to Shekhem, and south of Levonah.

20 And they commanded the people of Binyamin, saying, Go and lie in ambush in the vineyards

21 And watch. If the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in the dances, then come out of the vineyards and snatch each man his wife from the daughters of Shiloh, and go to the land of Binyamin.

22 And when their fathers or their brothers come to complain to us, we will say to them, Grant them graciously to us, because we did not take for each man of them his wife in battle, neither did you give them to them, else you would now be guilty.

23 And the people of Binyamin did so and took their wives, according to their number, from the dancers whom they carried off. Then they went and returned to their inheritance and rebuilt the towns and lived in them.

24 And the people of Yisra'EL departed from there at that time, every man to his tribe and family, and they went out from there every man to his inheritance.

25 In those days there was no king in Yisra'EL. Every man did what was right in his own eyes.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 21. The closing of Shofetim with the covenant people inventing increasingly desperate and morally compromised solutions — destroying Yavesh-Gil'ad, then condoning the abduction of the Shiloh dancers — is the covenant conclusion of the entire spiral of descent through the book.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 21 in full. Verse 25 — in those days there was no king in Yisra'EL — every man did what was right in his own eyes — is read as the final covenant diagnosis: the absence of covenant governance produces not freedom but the tyranny of unrestrained individual will.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 21. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The book of Shofetim opens with the covenant community asking who shall go up first to fight — Chapter 1:1. It closes with every man doing what is right in his own eyes. The descent is complete.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25 — every man did what was right in his own eyes. The closing refrain of Shofetim is not merely descriptive but diagnostic. What each individual considered right — each judge's personal code, each tribe's individual decision, each man's own assessment — became the operative covenant authority. The covenant given at Sinai, transmitted through Moshe, proclaimed by Yehoshua, was replaced by individual judgment. The result was not diverse flourishing but progressive collapse. The book of Shofetim is the covenant documentation of what happens when the covenant community loses the covenant as its shared reference: every man does what is right in his own eyes — and the outcomes accumulate into Giv'ah. Shofetim ends here, and Shemu'el Aleph begins with the answer to the question the book has posed: what must come to restore covenant order?

END OF PART THREE — CHAPTERS 15 THROUGH 21 — SHOFETIM COMPLETE
